topic: Do you actually need 8 glasses of water a day?

slides.headline: 8 glasses of water daily? That's a myth.
The '8x8 rule' has zero scientific backing
slides.subtext: No study actually supports drinking eight 8-ounce glasses daily for everyone.

slides.headline: Your actual needs depend on many factors
slides.subtext: Body size, activity level, climate, and health conditions all change your requirements.

slides.headline: Your body already tells you when hydrated
slides.subtext: Thirst is reliable. Pale yellow urine means you're doing fine already.

slides.headline: Food provides about 20% of water intake
slides.subtext: Fruits, vegetables, soups all count. You're not just relying on beverages.

slides.headline: Listen to your body, not arbitrary rules

caption:
I get asked about water intake constantly in clinic. Someone always mentions those famous '8 glasses a day' — but here's the truth: that number was never based on science.

Your hydration needs are as unique as you are. An office worker in air conditioning needs different amounts than a marathon runner in summer heat. Your kidneys are incredible at regulating fluid balance, and your brain has a built-in alert system called thirst.

The best hydration indicator? Your urine color. Aim for pale yellow. Dark? Drink more. Completely clear? You might be overdoing it.

And remember — coffee, tea, milk, and water-rich foods all count toward hydration. You don't need to force yourself to drink when you're not thirsty just to hit some arbitrary number.

Bottom line: trust your body's signals. Drink when thirsty. Adjust for exercise and heat. That's it.

#HydrationMyths #MedicalFacts #DoctorTips #EvidenceBasedMedicine #HealthMyths
